questions.question: Which of the following best describes the MRI signal characteristics of a neonatal brain compared to an adult brain?
choices: A. The T1 and T2 patterns are identical to an adult
B. The T1 pattern of a baby is similar to the T2 pattern of an adult
C. The T1 pattern of a baby is similar to the T1 pattern of an adult
D. The T2 pattern of a baby is similar to the T2 pattern of an adult
E. Neonatal brains show no grey-white differentiation
questions.detail: At birth, neonatal brain signal is essentially inverted compared to an adult: T1 is dark where it should be bright, and T2 is bright where it should be dark. [1, 2]

questions.question: Why does mature white matter appear brighter on T1-weighted images?
choices: A. Increase in total water content
C. Decrease in water and increase in fat (cholesterol and glycolipids)
D. Accumulation of iron in the myelin sheath
E. Presence of stagnant interstitial fluid
questions.detail: Myelination involves the replacement of water with cholesterol and glycolipids, which are T1-shortening (bright). [3, 4]

questions.question: At approximately what age is the 'adult T1 pattern' of myelination typically seen?
questions.detail: The '1 for T1' rule dictates that the adult T1 pattern is reached at 1 year of age. [3, 4]

questions.question: At approximately what age is the 'adult T2 pattern' of myelination typically seen?
questions.detail: The '2 for T2' rule dictates that the adult T2 pattern is reached at 2 years of age. [3, 4]

questions.question: Which MRI sequence is considered most useful for assessing myelination in an infant during the first 6 months of life?
questions.detail: T1-weighted imaging is most sensitive for tracking early myelination changes in the first 6 months. [4, 5]

questions.question: Which MRI sequence is most useful for assessing myelination progress between 6 months and 18 months of age?
questions.detail: T2-weighted imaging is the 'money' sequence for tracking progress after 6 months. [4, 5]

questions.question: Regarding the order of myelination progression, which of the following is correct?
choices: D. Sensory fibers prior to motor fibers
E. Subcortical white matter prior to deep white matter
questions.detail: Myelination typically follows a pattern of sensory before motor, inferior to superior, and posterior to anterior. [4, 5]

questions.question: Which anatomical area is the LAST part of the brain to undergo myelination?
choices: B. Posterior limb of the internal capsule
questions.detail: Subcortical white matter is the last major region to finish myelination. [4, 6]

questions.question: Myelination of the subcortical frontal regions is typically finished by what age?
questions.detail: Frontal subcortical white matter myelination finishes at roughly 18 months. [4, 6]

questions.question: The 'terminal zones' of myelination, located in the subcortical frontotemporoparietal regions, finish myelination around:
questions.detail: These specific 'terminal zones' can take up to 40 months to reach adult patterns. [4, 6]

questions.question: Which of the following structures is expected to be normally myelinated at the time of birth?
choices: C. Brainstem and posterior limb of the internal capsule
E. Anterior limb of the internal capsule
questions.detail: Essential structures for life, such as the brainstem and posterior limb of the internal capsule, are myelinated at birth. [4, 7]

questions.question: At birth, what are the expected T1 signal characteristics of the pituitary gland?
choices: A. Anterior is dark, posterior is bright
B. Both anterior and posterior are T1 bright
C. Both anterior and posterior are T1 iso-intense
D. The pituitary is not visible until 6 months
E. The gland is purely cystic at birth
questions.detail: In neonates, the entire pituitary gland is typically T1 bright. [4, 7]

questions.question: By what age does the anterior pituitary typically lose its T1 hyperintensity, becoming iso-intense?
questions.detail: The anterior gland loses its hyperintensity by 2 months of age. [4, 8]

questions.question: In a normal adult, which part of the pituitary gland is T1 bright?
questions.detail: In adults, only the posterior pituitary retains T1 hyperintensity. [4, 8]

questions.question: Why is the posterior pituitary T1 bright in an adult?
choices: B. Storage of Vasopressin and storage proteins
D. Ectopic fat from the hypothalamus
E. Artifact from the sphenoid sinus
questions.detail: The brightness results from stored neurosecretory granules containing Vasopressin. [4, 8]

questions.question: As a person ages, what change is expected in brain iron deposition?
choices: B. Iron increases, causing the globus pallidus to darken on T2/FLAIR
D. Iron only deposits in the cortex
E. Iron disappears from the substantia nigra
questions.detail: Accumulating brain iron results in signal dropout (darkening) of deep nuclei on T2/FLAIR with age. [4, 9]

questions.question: Calvarial bone marrow signal in a young child is typically 'active,' meaning it appears:
choices: E. Identical to adult fatty marrow
questions.detail: Active hematopoietic red marrow in children is T1 dark/hypointense. [4, 9]

questions.question: In what order do the paranasal sinuses typically form?
questions.detail: The developmental order is Maxillary (birth) -> Ethmoid (1yr) -> Sphenoid (4yrs) -> Frontal (6yrs). [4, 9]

questions.question: Which sinus is the first to be visible on a CT scan, often around 5 months of age?
questions.detail: The maxillary sinus is the first to become visible radiographically. [10, 11]

questions.question: By what age is the sphenoid sinus typically visible on CT?
questions.detail: The sphenoid sinus typically ossifies and is visible by age 4. [10, 11]

questions.question: The frontal sinus is generally the last to develop and is typically visible on CT around age:
questions.detail: The frontal sinus is usually not visible until age 6. [10, 11]

questions.question: Most paranasal sinuses finish forming by approximately what age?
questions.detail: Sinus development is generally complete by age 15. [10, 11]

questions.question: Congenital brain malformations can be broadly grouped into categories. Which of the following is NOT one of the 5 basic categories mentioned?
questions.detail: Failure to Vascularize is not listed as one of the primary embryological categories for malformations. [11, 12]

questions.question: In what order does the corpus callosum typically form during embryogenesis?
choices: A. Back to front (Splenium to Genu)
B. Front to back (Genu to Splenium), with the rostrum last
D. It forms simultaneously across its entire length
questions.detail: The CC forms front-to-back, except for the rostrum which forms last. [11, 12]

questions.question: If an MRI shows hypoplasia of the corpus callosum where the splenium is absent but the genu is intact, this is consistent with:
choices: A. A primary formation failure (Front-to-back rule)
questions.detail: Absence of the posterior components with intact anterior components follows the front-to-back formation rule. [11, 13]

questions.question: 'Colpocephaly' is a radiographic hallmark of corpus callosum agenesis and is defined as:
choices: A. Symmetric dilation of the frontal horns
B. Asymmetric dilation of the occipital horns
C. Complete absence of the lateral ventricles
E. Narrowing of the third ventricle
questions.detail: Colpocephaly is the teardrop-shaped dilation of the occipital horns. [11, 13]

questions.question: On a coronal MRI, the lateral ventricles in a patient with corpus callosum agenesis often have which characteristic appearance?
questions.detail: Upward pointing frontal horns create the 'steer horn' look on coronal views. [11, 14]

questions.question: On an axial MRI, the lateral ventricles in CC agenesis are described as 'Racing Car' ventricles because they are:
questions.detail: Parallel, widely spaced lateral ventricles are referred to as 'Racing Car' ventricles. [11, 14]

questions.question: What are 'Probst bundles'?
choices: B. Densely packed white matter tracts that run parallel to the midline
C. Draining veins in the cavernous sinus
D. Calcified nodules in tuberous sclerosis
E. Fragments of a ruptured dermoid
questions.detail: Probst bundles are white matter tracts that failed to cross the midline. [11, 14]

questions.question: Which malformation is considered the 'most common anomaly seen with other CNS malformations'?
questions.detail: Intracranial lipomas are the most common anomaly identified alongside other CNS defects. [11, 15]

questions.question: Where is the most common location for an intracranial lipoma?
questions.detail: The interhemispheric fissure is the #1 location (50% of cases). [11, 15]

questions.question: What is the second most common location for an intracranial lipoma?
questions.detail: The quadrigeminal cistern is the #2 location for intracranial lipomas. [15, 16]

questions.question: Radiologically, what is the most helpful sequence for identifying an intracranial lipoma?
questions.detail: Fat signal is bright on T1, making non-fat sat T1 the primary identification sequence. [16, 17]

questions.question: Which 'French sounding' phrase is used to explain the frequent pericallosal location of CNS lipomas?
choices: B. Maldifferentiation of the Meninx Primitiva
questions.detail: This is the embryological term for the failure of meninx differentiation. [16, 17]

questions.question: Joubert Syndrome is characterized by which classic appearance of the midbrain on axial imaging?
questions.detail: Elongated superior cerebellar peduncles create the 'molar tooth' look. [16, 17]

questions.question: In Joubert syndrome, the 'molar tooth' appearance is caused by:
choices: A. Thickened and elongated superior cerebellar peduncles
B. Absence of the pituitary gland
C. Fusion of the frontal lobes
D. End-stage calcification of the basal ganglia
E. Enlargement of the red nuclei
questions.detail: The midbrain peduncles are abnormally long and thickened. [16, 18]

questions.question: Which of the following is a consistent feature of Joubert syndrome?
choices: B. Small cerebellum and a 'batwing' shaped 4th ventricle
D. Absence of the falx cerebri
questions.detail: Hypoplasia of the vermis creates the 'batwing' fourth ventricle. [16, 18]

questions.question: Which two markers of normal vermian development should a radiologist look for on a mid-sagittal image?
choices: B. Primary fissure and fastigial point
D. Foramen of Monro and Aqueduct
questions.detail: The primary fissure and fastigial point must be present to confirm a normal vermis. [16, 19]

questions.question: The 'fastigial point' is the normal angular contour found along the:
choices: A. Dorsal surface of the midbrain
B. Ventral surface of the cerebellum
C. Superior margin of the pons
D. Lateral wall of the 3rd ventricle
questions.detail: It is the angular point on the ventral cerebellar surface/roof of the 4th ventricle. [16, 19]

questions.question: A 'Classic' Dandy Walker malformation consists of a triad of findings. Which of the following is NOT part of that triad?
choices: D. Absence of the corpus callosum
questions.detail: The triad includes vermian hypoplasia, cystic 4th ventricle, and large posterior fossa. [16, 19]

questions.question: On axial imaging, a Dandy Walker malformation can mimic which other entity?
questions.detail: Retrocerebellar cysts and mega cisterna magna are standard mimics. [16, 20]

questions.question: The 'Torcular-lambdoid inversion' in Dandy Walker describes:
choices: A. The torcula being below the level of the lambdoid suture
B. The torcula being above the level of the lambdoid suture
C. Absence of the transverse sinuses
D. Fusion of the sigmoid sinuses
questions.detail: The confluence of sinuses (torcula) is displaced superiorly in Dandy-Walker. [16, 20]

questions.question: What is the most common clinical manifestation of a Dandy Walker malformation in the first month of life?
questions.detail: Hydrocephalus leads to presenting macrocephaly in nearly all neonates. [16, 20]

questions.question: Which associated CNS malformation is seen in approximately 90% of Dandy Walker cases?
questions.detail: Almost all Dandy-Walker cases feature associated hydrocephalus. [16, 21]

questions.question: Holoprosencephaly (HPE) is a failure of what embryologic process?
choices: B. Cleavage of the prosencephalon into two hemispheres
C. Formation of the neural tube
D. Organization of the cortical layers
E. Closure of the choroid fissure
questions.detail: HPE is the failure of the single brain vesicle to divide into two. [16, 21]

questions.question: Which form of Holoprosencephaly is the most severe, showing zero midline cleavage and a single midline ventricle?
questions.detail: Alobar HPE features total lack of cleavage and a monoventricle. [21, 22]

questions.question: In the 'Semilobar' form of HPE, what percentage of the frontal lobes are fused?
questions.detail: Semilobar features > 50% fusion of the frontal lobes. [22, 23]

questions.question: Which facial abnormality is classically associated with Holoprosencephaly?
questions.detail: Midline facial defects like clefts are frequently associated with HPE. [22, 23]

questions.question: Septo-optic Dysplasia (SOD) is characterized by the absence of which structure?
questions.detail: Absence of the septum pellucidum is the defining hallmark of SOD. [22, 23]

questions.question: Besides the absent septum pellucidum, SOD involves hypoplasia of which specific structures?
choices: A. Optic chiasm and optic nerves
questions.detail: Optic pathway hypoplasia is the second requirement for diagnosis. [22, 23]

questions.question: Which rare vascular variant is associated with both Septo-optic Dysplasia and lobar Holoprosencephaly?
choices: C. Azygos anterior cerebral artery (common trunk)
questions.detail: The Azygos ACA is strongly associated with midline cleavage defects. [22, 24]

questions.question: The brain is said to form 'inside-out,' meaning neurons are birthed from which region?
questions.detail: Neurons originate at the ventricular surface (the Pit) and migrate outward. [22, 24]

questions.question: How many 'waves' of neuronal cells typically make the climb to freedom during migration?
questions.detail: The 6-layered cortex is formed by 6 successive waves of migrating neurons. [22, 25]

questions.question: In the 'Hemimegalencephaly' vs 'Atrophy' strategy, if you see a BIG hemisphere with a BIG ventricle, it is:
questions.detail: A big side with a big ventricle is pathognomonic for hemimegalencephaly. [22, 25]

questions.question: If you see a SMALL hemisphere with a BIG ventricle, it is:
questions.detail: A small side with a big ventricle (ex-vacuo) indicates atrophy. [22, 25]

questions.question: 'Rasmussen Encephalitis' is a zebra viral/autoimmune disease that characteristically:
choices: A. Causes overgrowth of one hemisphere
B. Annihilates half the brain, resulting in atrophy and ex-vacuo dilation
E. Always spares the temporal lobes
questions.detail: Rasmussen is a destructive process resulting in progressive hemi-atrophy. [22, 25]

questions.question: Dyke-Davidoff-Masson (Cerebral Hemi-Atrophy) is distinguished from Rasmussen because it also includes:
choices: B. Unilateral skull thickening and expanded sinuses
questions.detail: Bone and sinus changes distinguish it from primary encephalitic atrophy. [22, 26]

questions.question: Which term refers to the 'classic' Type 1 Lissencephaly characterized by a smooth brain surface?
questions.detail: Agyria (no gyri) is the characteristic feature of Type 1 Lissencephaly. [22, 26]

questions.question: On axial imaging, a 'Classic' Type 1 Lissencephaly often has which shape due to shallow Sylvan fissures?
questions.detail: The 'Figure 8' or 'hourglass' shape is classic. [22, 26]

questions.question: Type 1 Lissencephaly is a failure of ________ migration.
questions.detail: Type 1 is a failure of undermigration (neurons don't reach the surface). [22, 27]

questions.question: Type 2 'Cobblestone' Lissencephaly is the result of ________ migration.
questions.detail: Type 2 involves overmigration through the glia limitans into the subarachnoid space. [22, 27]

questions.question: 'Muscle-eye-brain disease' is an association found in which condition?
questions.detail: This disease is a standard association for Cobblestone Lissencephaly. [22, 27]

questions.question: Where is the most common location for grey matter heterotopia?
questions.detail: Heterotopias are most common in the subependymal periventricular region. [22, 27]

questions.question: Periventricular Nodular Heterotopias are highly associated with which clinical symptom?
questions.detail: Refractory seizures are the classic presentation for heterotopias. [22, 28]

questions.question: How can you radiographically distinguish a heterotopia from a subependymal tuber of Tuberous Sclerosis?
choices: A. Heterotopias follow grey matter and do NOT enhance
questions.detail: Heterotopias follow grey matter on all sequences and never enhance, unlike tubers. [28, 29]

questions.question: Polymicrogyria (PMG) is a failure of organization that results in:
choices: B. An excessive number of small, fine folds (bumpy cortex)
questions.detail: PMG is defined by an overabundance of tiny, malformed gyri. [28, 29]

questions.question: Which virus is the most common cause of PMG in Brazil and South America?
questions.detail: CMV is the leading infectious cause of PMG. [29, 30]

questions.question: Schizencephaly literally means 'split brain' and is defined by a cleft lined with:
questions.detail: The hallmark of schizencephaly is a cleft lined by dysplastic grey matter. [29, 30]

questions.question: Which type of schizencephaly is 'more common and more severe'?
questions.detail: Open lip schizencephaly is the most common and clinically severe subtype. [29, 30]

questions.question: In 'Closed Lip' schizencephaly, what is the subtle clue on the ependymal (ventricular) surface?
choices: B. A 'nipple' of grey matter pouching
E. Absence of the choroid plexus
questions.detail: The 'ependymal nipple' is the subtle sign for closed-lip clefts. [29, 30]

questions.question: Which condition is associated with Schizencephaly in 70% of cases?
questions.detail: Absence of the septum pellucidum is a standard co-occurrence with schizencephaly. [29, 31]

questions.question: How do you distinguish Schizencephaly from Porencephaly?
choices: A. Schizencephaly is lined with grey matter; Porencephaly is not
B. Porencephaly is congenital; Schizencephaly is acquired
C. Schizencephaly is only seen in adults
D. Porencephaly always crosses the midline
questions.detail: Grey matter lining the defect confirms schizencephaly over porencephaly. [29, 31]

questions.question: Hydranencephaly is a massive in-utero destruction of both cerebral hemispheres, typically leaving only the:
choices: C. Cerebellum, midbrain, and the falx
questions.detail: Posterior fossa structures and the falx are typically preserved. [29, 31]

questions.question: The most classic cause of Hydranencephaly is:
questions.detail: Massive destruction follows bilateral occlusion of the internal carotid arteries. [2, 29]

questions.question: To differentiate severe hydrocephalus from Alobar Holoprosencephaly, you look for:
choices: A. The size of the thalami
B. The presence of the falx cerebri
E. The size of the cerebellum
questions.detail: The presence of a falx cerebri confirms hydrocephalus and rules out alobar HPE. [2, 29]

questions.question: An incredibly creepy 'Frog Eye' appearance on a coronal fetal MRI (due to absent cranial bone/brain with bulging orbits) is the hallmark of which condition?
questions.detail: Anencephaly creates the 'frog eye' appearance due to absent cranial vault and brain tissue. [32, 33]

questions.question: In the setting of Anencephaly, which finding is typically identified on antenatal ultrasound because the fetus cannot swallow properly?
questions.detail: Polyhydramnios occurs because swallowing mechanisms are absent or impaired. [32, 33]

questions.question: Which lab value is consistently elevated in all 'Open Neural Tube Defects'?
questions.detail: AFP leaks from the open defect into the amniotic fluid and maternal serum. [32, 33]

questions.question: A 'Star Gazing Fetus,' characterized by extreme retroflexion of the head and a deficient occipital bone, is diagnostic of:
questions.detail: Iniencephaly is defined by extreme retroflexion and occipital bone defects. [33, 34]

questions.question: What is the definition of a 'Cephalocele'?
choices: A. Herniation of the cerebellum into the spinal canal
B. Herniation of cranial contents through a defect in the skull
C. Failure of the prosencephalon to cleave
D. A CSF-filled space between the frontal horns
questions.detail: A cephalocele is a general term for any herniation through a skull defect. [33, 34]

questions.question: Where is the 'most classic' location for an intracranial encephalocele?
questions.detail: The occipital region is the most frequent site for encephaloceles. [35, 36]

questions.question: A herniation sac containing only CSF and meninges, but 'NO BRAIN,' is specifically called a:
questions.detail: A meningocele contains only CSF and meninges without neural tissue. [35, 36]

questions.question: An occipital encephalocele is most classically associated with which other malformation?
questions.detail: Chiari III is essentially an occipital encephalocele containing posterior fossa contents. [35, 36]

questions.question: According to the 'testable vocab' for encephaloceles, a 'Myelocele' contains:
questions.detail: A myelocele specifically contains spinal cord tissue. [36, 37]

questions.question: A 'Chiari I' malformation is defined by the herniation of the cerebellar tonsils below the foramen magnum by more than:
questions.detail: The diagnostic threshold for tonsillar herniation is 5 mm. [36, 37]

questions.question: Which condition is the most classic (though not always present) association with a Chiari I malformation?
questions.detail: A cervical cord syrinx is frequently identified in Chiari I patients. [36, 37]

questions.question: 'Tectal beaking,' clival hypoplasia, and a low-lying torcula are classic features of which malformation?
questions.detail: Tectal beaking and torcular displacement are hallmarks of Chiari II. [36, 38]

questions.question: In Chiari II, the confluence of venous sinuses (torcula) is typically:
choices: D. Located in the frontal bone
questions.detail: The torcula is characteristically low-lying in Chiari II. [36, 38]

questions.question: Which hybrid term describes a condition with features of both Type 1 and Type 2 Chiari, but is notably NOT associated with neural tube defects?
questions.detail: Chiari 1.5 features tonsillar and brainstem herniation but no open neural tube defect. [36, 38]

questions.question: Rhombencephalosynapsis is an 'Aunt Minnie' diagnosis characterized by the 'ABSENCE' of which structure?
questions.detail: Total absence of the vermis results in cerebellar hemisphere fusion. [36, 39]

questions.question: The absence of the vermis in Rhombencephalosynapsis results in what characteristic appearance of the cerebellum?
choices: C. Transversely oriented single lobed cerebellum (fused)
questions.detail: Fused hemispheres create a single, continuous transverse cerebellar lobe. [36, 39]

questions.question: When assessing the vermis on a mid-sagittal image, what is the 'fastigial point'?
choices: A. The most anterior part of the pons
B. A normal angular contour along the ventral surface of the cerebellum
C. The opening of the aqueduct
D. The apex of the medulla
E. The midpoint of the corpus callosum
questions.detail: It is the angular point on the ventral cerebellar surface/roof of the 4th ventricle. [36, 40]

questions.question: On the spectrum of Dandy Walker and friends, which entity is considered a 'Normal Variant' and shows a focal enlargement of the retrocerebellar CSF space with a normal vermis?
questions.detail: Mega cisterna magna features an enlarged posterior fossa with a preserved vermis. [36, 40]

questions.question: A 'Blake Pouch Cyst' is a sac-like protrusion that only communicates with:
questions.detail: A Blake pouch cyst communicates only with the ventricular system, unlike other posterior fossa cysts. [36, 41]

questions.question: A 'Classic' Dandy Walker malformation consists of a triad. Which of the following is NOT part of that triad?
choices: D. Absence of the septum pellucidum
questions.detail: Septum pellucidum absence is associated with HPE and SOD, not DWM. [36, 41]

questions.question: 'Torcular-lambdoid inversion' is a buzzword for Dandy Walker describing:
choices: A. The torcula being below the level of the lambdoid suture
B. The torcula being above the level of the lambdoid suture
C. Absence of the lambdoid suture
D. Fusion of the confluence of sinuses
E. A normal finding in 100% of infants
questions.detail: The confluence of sinuses (torcula) is displaced superiorly in Dandy-Walker. [36, 41]

questions.question: What is the most common clinical manifestation of Dandy Walker, seen in nearly all cases within the first month of life?
questions.detail: Hydrocephalus leads to presenting macrocephaly in nearly all neonates. [36, 42]

questions.question: Hydrocephalus is associated with Classic Dandy Walker in what percentage of cases?
questions.detail: Almost all Dandy-Walker cases feature associated hydrocephalus. [36, 42]

questions.question: Holoprosencephaly (HPE) is a failure of the prosencephalon to:
questions.detail: HPE is the failure of the single brain vesicle to divide into two. [36, 43]

questions.question: Which form of HPE is the 'most severe,' showing zero midline cleavage and a single midline ventricle?
questions.detail: Alobar HPE features total lack of cleavage and a monoventricle. [36, 43]

questions.question: In Alobar Holoprosencephaly, the thalami are typically:
choices: D. Located in the posterior fossa
questions.detail: Fused thalami are a characteristic feature of severe HPE. [36, 43]

questions.question: In the 'Semilobar' form of HPE, what is the extent of frontal lobe fusion?
questions.detail: Semilobar features > 50% fusion of the frontal lobes. [36, 44]

questions.question: Which facial abnormality is a classic 'multiple choice association' for Holoprosencephaly?
questions.detail: Midline facial defects like clefts are frequently associated with HPE. [36, 44]

questions.question: 'Septo-optic Dysplasia (SOD)' is defined by the absence of the septum pellucidum and hypoplasia of which structures?
choices: B. Optic chiasm and optic nerves
questions.detail: Optic pathway hypoplasia is the second requirement for diagnosis. [44, 45]

questions.question: Which rare vascular variant, characterized by a single common trunk of the ACAs, is associated with SOD and lobar HPE?
questions.detail: The Azygos ACA is strongly associated with midline cleavage defects. [45, 46]

questions.question: The brain is said to form 'inside-out.' From which region are the neurons originally birthed?
questions.detail: Neurons originate at the ventricular surface (the Pit) and migrate outward. [45, 46]

questions.question: During the migration phase of cortical formation, neurons make the 'climb' to the cortex in how many waves?
questions.detail: The 6-layered cortex is formed by 6 successive waves of migrating neurons. [45, 46]

questions.question: In the 'Inside-Out' theory of migration, which cells are found most superficially in their final position?
choices: B. The younger cells (moving past older ones)
C. Cells with the least myelin
questions.detail: Newer cells migrate past older ones to reach the outer cortical layers. [45, 47]

questions.question: What is the key mechanism for brain folding (gyrification)?
choices: C. Differential speed of cortex expansion relative to deeper white matter
D. Traction from the cranial nerves
questions.detail: Folding results from the faster growth rate of the cortical surface compared to the core. [45, 47]

questions.question: In the 'Hemimegalencephaly vs Atrophy' strategy, how do you identify Hemimegalencephaly?
choices: A. Small side with a BIG ventricle
B. BIG side with a BIG ventricle
C. BIG side with a SMALL ventricle
D. Symmetric sides with NO ventricles
E. Small side with a SMALL ventricle
questions.detail: A big side with a big ventricle is pathognomonic for hemimegalencephaly. [45, 47]

questions.question: 'Hamartomatous Overgrowth' of an entire cerebral hemisphere is the definition of:
questions.detail: Hemimegalencephaly is a hamartomatous overgrowth of half the brain. [45, 48]

questions.question: A 'SMALL side with a BIG ventricle' is the result of:
questions.detail: A small side with a big ventricle (ex-vacuo) indicates atrophy. [45, 48]

questions.question: Rasmussen’s Encephalitis is a destructive process that characteristically:
choices: A. Affects the entire brain symmetrically
B. 'Annihilates half the brain,' resulting in atrophy and ex-vacuo dilation
C. Results in massive hamartomatous overgrowth
D. Is only seen in the elderly
questions.detail: Rasmussen is a destructive process resulting in progressive hemi-atrophy. [45, 48]

questions.question: Dyke-Davidoff-Masson (Cerebral Hemi-atrophy) is distinguished from Rasmussen because it also includes which feature?
choices: A. Unilateral skull thickening and expanded sinuses
questions.detail: Bone and sinus changes distinguish it from primary encephalitic atrophy. [45, 49]

questions.question: 'Classic' Type 1 Lissencephaly is a failure of:
questions.detail: Type 1 is a failure of undermigration (neurons don't reach the surface). [45, 50]

questions.question: A 'Figure 8' shaped brain on axial imaging due to shallow, vertical Sylvian fissures is characteristic of:
questions.detail: The 'Figure 8' or 'hourglass' shape is classic for Type 1. [45, 50]

questions.question: In Type 1 Lissencephaly, the cortex is 'Thick' because it is composed of how many layers instead of the normal 6?
questions.detail: Type 1 Lissencephaly typically features a 4-layered thickened cortex. [45, 50]

questions.question: Which condition is considered the 'mildest form' of Classic Lissencephaly and creates a 'double cortex' appearance?
questions.detail: Band heterotopia is often described as a 'double cortex'. [45, 50]

questions.question: Band Heterotopia (Double Cortex) is most common in which gender?
questions.detail: This specific migration defect is far more frequent in females. [45, 51]

questions.question: 'Cobblestone' Type 2 Lissencephaly is the result of:
questions.detail: Type 2 involves overmigration through the glia limitans into the subarachnoid space. [45, 51]

questions.question: 'Muscle-eye-brain disease' is an association classically found in which condition?
questions.detail: This disease is a standard association for Cobblestone Lissencephaly. [45, 51]

questions.question: Where is the most common location for 'Grey Matter Heterotopia'?
questions.detail: Heterotopias are most common in the subependymal periventricular region. [45, 52]

questions.question: Periventricular Nodular Heterotopias are highly associated with which clinical symptom?
questions.detail: Refractory seizures are the classic presentation for heterotopias. [45, 52]

questions.question: How can a radiologist distinguish a heterotopia from a subependymal nodule of Tuberous Sclerosis?
choices: A. Heterotopias follow grey matter on all sequences and do NOT enhance
B. Subependymal nodules follow CSF signal
D. Subependymal nodules are T1 bright
questions.detail: Heterotopias follow grey matter on all sequences and never enhance, unlike tubers. [52, 53]

questions.question: Polymicrogyria (PMG) is a failure of 'Organization' that results in:
choices: B. An excessive number of small, fine folds (bumpy cortex)
questions.detail: PMG is defined by an overabundance of tiny, malformed gyri. [53, 54]

questions.question: Which TORCH infection is most strongly associated with Polymicrogyria?
questions.detail: CMV is the leading infectious cause of PMG. [53, 54]

questions.question: Schizencephaly is defined as a brain cleft or hole that is 'LINED WITH':
questions.detail: The hallmark of schizencephaly is a cleft lined by dysplastic grey matter. [53, 55]

questions.question: Which type of Schizencephaly is described as 'more common and more severe'?
questions.detail: Open lip schizencephaly is the most common and clinically severe subtype. [53, 55]

questions.question: In 'Closed Lip' schizencephaly, what subtle clue might be seen on the ependymal (ventricular) surface?
choices: B. A 'nipple' of grey matter pouching
questions.detail: The 'ependymal nipple' is the subtle sign for closed-lip clefts. [53, 55]

questions.question: Absent septum pellucidum is associated with Schizencephaly in what percentage of cases?
questions.detail: Absence of the septum pellucidum is a standard co-occurrence with schizencephaly. [53, 56]

questions.question: How do you definitively distinguish Schizencephaly from Porencephaly?
choices: A. Schizencephaly is lined with grey matter; Porencephaly is NOT
B. Porencephaly is congenital; Schizencephaly is acquired
C. Schizencephaly crosses the midline; Porencephaly does not
questions.detail: Grey matter lining the defect confirms schizencephaly over porencephaly. [53, 56]

questions.question: Porencephalic cysts are 'acquired,' classically resulting from:
choices: B. A prior ischemic or traumatic event (encephalomalacia)
questions.detail: Porencephalic cysts represent areas of prior brain tissue destruction. [53, 57]

questions.question: 'Hydranencephaly' is a massive in-utero destruction of both cerebral hemispheres, typically leaving only the:
choices: C. Cerebellum, midbrain, and the falx
questions.detail: Posterior fossa structures and the falx are typically preserved. [53, 57]

questions.question: What is the most classic suspected cause of Hydranencephaly?
questions.detail: Massive destruction follows bilateral occlusion of the internal carotid arteries. [53, 57]

questions.question: To differentiate 'Severe Hydrocephalus' from 'Alobar Holoprosencephaly,' you should look for:
choices: A. The size of the cerebellum
B. The presence of the falx cerebri (present in hydrocephalus)
C. The intensity of the CSF
D. The presence of the pituitary gland
questions.detail: The presence of a falx cerebri confirms hydrocephalus and rules out alobar HPE. [53, 58]

questions.question: In Alobar HPE, the anterior falx cerebri is typically:
questions.detail: The anterior falx is typically absent in Alobar HPE. [53, 58]

questions.question: Which 'historical term' was used to describe severe cerebellar hypoplasia without herniation, but has since fallen out of favor?
questions.detail: Chiari IV was historically used for severe hypoplasia without displacement. [53, 59]

questions.question: In Dandy Walker, the cerebellar hemispheres are displaced in which direction?
questions.detail: The expanding 4th ventricle monoventricle pushes hemispheres forward. [53, 59]

questions.question: 'Probst bundles' are white matter tracts destined to cross the corpus callosum that instead run parallel to the midline. They are diagnostic for:
questions.detail: Probst bundles are white matter tracts that failed to cross the midline. [53, 59]

questions.question: On axial imaging, lateral ventricles that are 'widely spaced and parallel' (Racing Car appearance) suggest:
questions.detail: Parallel, widely spaced lateral ventricles are referred to as 'Racing Car' ventricles. [53, 60]

questions.question: On coronal imaging, lateral ventricles with a 'steer horn' appearance suggest:
questions.detail: Upward pointing frontal horns create the 'steer horn' look on coronal views. [53, 60]

questions.question: A cephalocele sac containing the spinal cord is a:
questions.detail: A myelocele specifically contains spinal cord tissue. [53, 60]

questions.question: Blake Pouch Cysts do NOT communicate with which space?
questions.detail: Blake pouch cysts are ventricular diverticula and do not communicate with the subarachnoid space. [61, 62]

questions.question: In Dandy Walker, the posterior fossa is typically:
questions.detail: An expanded posterior fossa is a cardinal feature of DWM. [61, 62]

questions.question: Failure of the prosencephalon to divide into hemispheres results in a spectrum of disorders known as:
questions.detail: HPE represents the failure of primary brain vesicle cleavage. [61, 62]

questions.question: Facial findings in Holoprosencephaly can include 'Borat’s brother Bilo,' which is a crude mnemonic for:
questions.detail: Clefts are the hallmark facial association for HPE. [62, 63]

questions.question: An 'Azygos Anterior Cerebral Artery' is basically a:
choices: C. Common trunk of the ACAs
D. Connection to the basilar artery
E. Vein that mimics an artery
questions.detail: The Azygos ACA variant features a single shared ACA trunk. [62, 63]

questions.question: The 'differential speed of cortex expansion' relative to white matter is the mechanism behind:
questions.detail: Gyrification occurs because the cortex expands faster than the underlying core. [62, 63]

questions.question: Lissencephaly Type 1 is a failure of neuronal:
questions.detail: Type 1 results from neurons failing to complete their outward climb. [62, 63]

questions.question: Do grey matter heterotopias enhance with Gadolinium?
choices: B. Yes, but only in the acute phase
D. Only if associated with cancer
questions.detail: Heterotopias are composed of normal neural tissue and do not enhance. [62, 63]
